elements.sent-2411-001: Anomaly detection (AD) is a machine learning task that identifies anomalies by learning patterns from normal training data.
elements.sent-2411-002: In many real-world scenarios, anomalies vary in severity, from minor anomalies with little risk to severe abnormalities requiring immediate attention.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, existing models primarily operate in a binary setting, and the anomaly scores they produce are usually based on the deviation of data points from normal data, which may not accurately reflect practical severity.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we address this gap by making three key contributions.
elements.sent-2411-005: First, we propose a novel setting, Multilevel AD (MAD), in which the anomaly score represents the severity of anomalies in real-world applications, and we highlight its diverse applications across various domains.
elements.sent-2411-006: Second, we introduce a novel benchmark, MAD-Bench, that evaluates models not only on their ability to detect anomalies, but also on how effectively their anomaly scores reflect severity.
elements.sent-2411-007: This benchmark incorporates multiple types of baselines and real-world applications involving severity.
elements.sent-2411-008: Finally, we conduct a comprehensive performance analysis on MAD-Bench.
elements.sent-2411-009: We evaluate models on their ability to assign severity-aligned scores, investigate the correspondence between their performance on binary and multilevel detection, and study their robustness.
elements.sent-2411-010: This analysis offers key insights into improving AD models for practical severity alignment.
elements.sent-2411-011: The code framework and datasets used for the benchmark will be made publicly available.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{figs/intro6.jpg} \caption{ (a) Binary Anomaly Detection classifies data as either in-distribution (ID) or out-of-distribution (OOD), without accounting for severity. (b) The Multilevel Anomaly Detection setting categorizes OOD data by severity, reflecting the potential impact or risk. For instance, in COVID-19 chest X-rays, severity increases with greater lung involvement, from mild ground-glass opacities to extensive consolidation, indicating heightened clinical urgency.} \label{fig:intro}
elements.sent-2411-012: Anomaly detection (AD) is a fundamental machine learning task that identifies deviations from normal patterns, with critical applications in domains such as one-class novelty detection \cite{ruff2018deep, chen2022deep , industrial inspection \cite{bergmann2019mvtec, zou2022spot , and medical diagnostics \cite{schlegl2019f, shvetsova2021anomaly .}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, in many real-world scenarios, anomalies are not uniformly significant; they exist on a spectrum of severity, ranging from minor anomalies that pose little risk to severe abnormalities that demand immediate attention.
elements.sent-2411-014: In a practical context, severity refers to the degree of potential impact or risk an anomaly poses to the system.
elements.sent-2411-015: For instance, in industrial inspection, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:intro b, anomalies range from minor surface contaminations to severe tears, requiring different levels of response.}
elements.sent-2411-016: The ability to differentiate between these levels of severity is important for effective decision-making, prioritizing critical issues, and resource allocation.
elements.sent-2411-017: While distinguishing between different levels of anomaly severity is crucial, it remains unclear whether current anomaly detection methods are truly effective in capturing this distinction.
elements.sent-2411-018: Existing approaches typically focus on ensuring that the anomaly scores of anomalous data are higher than those of normal samples, operating primarily in a binary setting.
elements.sent-2411-019: Current techniques, such as reconstruction-based \cite{park2020learning,yan2021learning,zavrtanik2021reconstruction,pourreza2021g2,zaheer2020old , one-class classifiers \cite{chen2022deep,goyal2020drocc,wu2019deep,sabokrou2020deep,yi2020patch , and knowledge distillation-based models \cite{salehi2021multiresolution, wang2021student, deng2022anomaly, tien2023revisiting , rely on metrics like reconstruction errors, distance measures, or likelihood estimates to flag deviations from normal patterns.}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, the anomaly scores produced by these models are typically based on how far a data point deviates from normal data, but it is uncertain how well these scores correlate with the practical severity of the anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-021: These observations raise a key question: How accurately can the anomaly scores measure practical severity? To the best of our knowledge, there are no works that directly address this question.
elements.sent-2411-022: While near-distribution novelty detection \cite{mirzaei2022fake is related, it focuses on distinguishing between normal samples and abnormal samples that closely resemble normal ones rather than investigating the relationship between anomaly scores and severity.}
elements.sent-2411-023: In this paper, we aim to study the relationship between anomaly score and severity of anomalies by presenting three main contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-024: First, we propose a novel setting, Multilevel Anomaly Detection (MAD), where the anomaly score should represent the severity of anomalies in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-025: We highlight the applications of multilevel anomaly detection in real-world scenarios, including one-class novelty detection, anomaly detection in medical imaging, and industrial inspection.
elements.sent-2411-026: Building on the MAD setting, we introduce a new benchmark named MAD-Bench, designed to evaluate models on both their anomaly detection capabilities and their effectiveness in assigning severity-aligned anomaly scores.
elements.sent-2411-027: To achieve this, we adapt existing datasets from diverse domains to the multilevel anomaly detection context.
elements.sent-2411-028: In addition to conventional baselines, we incorporate Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM)-based baselines, which leverage domain knowledge and reasoning abilities to assign anomaly scores.
elements.sent-2411-029: This makes MAD-Bench a comprehensive framework for evaluating model performance in MAD settings.
elements.sent-2411-030: Finally, we conduct a comprehensive performance analysis using MAD-Bench, evaluating models on their ability to assign severity-aligned anomaly scores, the correlation between binary and multilevel detection performance, and the impact of anomalous feature area.
elements.sent-2411-031: We also assess performance across severity levels, the effect of including light anomalies in the normal class, and robustness under input corruption.
elements.sent-2411-032: These insights help improve AD models for practical severity alignment.
elements.sent-2411-033: Many approaches have been proposed for anomaly detection.
elements.sent-2411-034: One-class classification methods have progressed from machine learning approaches \cite{scholkopf2001estimating, tax2004support to deep learning-based techniques \cite{ruff2018deep, chen2022deep, goyal2020drocc, wu2019deep, sabokrou2020deep, yi2020patch, liu2023simplenet , which improve boundary refinement for distinguishing normal and anomalous data.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Reconstruction-based models \cite{yan2021learning, zavrtanik2021reconstruction, pourreza2021g2, zaheer2020old, zavrtanik2022dsr, liang2022omni, zavrtanik2021draem identify anomalies by measuring deviations in reconstruction errors.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Additionally, self-supervised learning techniques \cite{li2021cutpaste, yan2021learning, zhang2024realnet, schluter2022natural introduce synthetic anomalies to enhance model differentiation, improving detection accuracy in complex scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Knowledge distillation-based methods employ a teacher-student framework \cite{bergmann2020uninformed, salehi2021multiresolution, wang2021student, deng2022anomaly, tien2023revisiting, gu2023remembering, zhang2023destseg , where anomalies are detected based on deviations between teacher and student representations.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Distribution map-based methods \cite{gudovskiy2022cflow, shin2023anomaly, Lei_2023_CVPR model the normal data distribution and identify outliers as deviations.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Memory-based approaches \cite{roth2022towards, bae2023pni, gu2023remembering, gong2019memorizing, hou2021divide, park2020learning, wang2021student detect anomalies by comparing input features to representative normal data stored in a memory bank.. MLLM-Based Approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-040: MLLMs have recently been leveraged for anomaly detection due to their ability to handle multimodal reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-041: These approaches often utilize zero-shot learning \cite{anovl, Jiang2024MMADTF or few-shot learning \cite{li2024promptad, zhu2024toward , allowing them to adapt to new datasets without extensive task-specific training.. AD Benchmarks.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Several benchmarks for anomaly detection have been introduced \cite{Jiang2024MMADTF, ader, xie2024iad, akcay2022anomalib, han2022adbench, wang2024real, cao2023anomaly .}
elements.sent-2411-043: However, these benchmarks primarily focus on traditional binary anomaly detection rather than addressing more nuanced multilevel anomaly detection settings.
elements.sent-2411-044: Near out-of-distribution (OOD) detection aims to identify anomalies that are similar to normal data, showing only slight deviations \cite{mirzaei2022fake .}
elements.sent-2411-045: This differs from our work, which explores the relationship between anomaly scores and varying levels of severity.
elements.sent-2411-046: Multi-class AD identifies anomalies across various object classes within a single, unified framework \cite{you2022unified, he2024diffusion, lu2023hierarchical, ader .}
elements.sent-2411-047: By training on multiple normal classes, it identifies anomalies as instances deviating from any learned normal patterns.
elements.sent-2411-048: In contrast, Multilevel AD focuses on representing multiple levels of anomalies during inference and ensures that anomaly scores reflect these levels.
elements.sent-2411-049: In anomaly detection, the goal is to define a function \( f: \mathcal{X \to \mathbb{R \) that assigns an anomaly score \( f(x) \) to each data point \( x \in \mathcal{X \), where higher scores indicate a greater likelihood of being an anomaly.}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Traditional approaches typically treat anomalies in a binary manner, detecting whether a point is normal or anomalous.
elements.sent-2411-051: However, real-world anomalies often vary in severity rather than fitting into binary categories.
elements.sent-2411-052: For example, in medical diagnostics, doctors assess abnormalities on a spectrum, from mild issues needing observation to critical conditions requiring urgent care.
elements.sent-2411-053: Motivated by this, we propose a Multilevel Anomaly Detection (MAD) setting, where data points are from \( L_0 \) to \( L_n \), with \( L_0 \) representing the set of normal data and \( L_1, L_2, \dots, L_n \) representing sets of increasingly severe levels of anomaly.
elements.sent-2411-054: Let the training set be defined as \( \mathcal{D _{\text{train \subseteq L_0 \), consisting exclusively of normal data points from \( L_0 \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: The testing set is defined as \( \mathcal{D _{\text{test \subseteq L_0 \cup L_1 \cup \dots \cup L_n \), which includes data from all levels \( L_0, L_1, \dots, L_n \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: The goal is to design an anomaly scoring function \( f(x) \) such that higher severity levels correspond to higher anomaly scores.
elements.sent-2411-057: Specifically, normal data points in \( L_0 \) should be assigned the lowest anomaly scores \( f(x_0) \), while data points from higher severity levels \( L_1, L_2, \dots, L_n \) should receive correspondingly higher scores.
elements.sent-2411-058: This ensures a consistent and interpretable mapping between anomaly severity and anomaly scores.
elements.sent-2411-059: In this section, we outline the evaluation protocol used to assess the performance of models on the multi-level anomaly detection task.
elements.sent-2411-060: Three key metrics will be employed: AUROC \cite{hanley1982meaning , C-index \cite{uno2011c , and Kendall’s Tau-b \cite{kendall1938new .}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: The AUROC metric is used to evaluate the model’s ability to distinguish between normal data and anomalies, which is commonly used in binary AD settings \cite{chen2022deep, cao2023anomaly .}
elements.sent-2411-062: The C-index is a generalization of the AUROC that can evaluate how well anomaly scores align with the severity levels.
elements.sent-2411-063: In the context of MAD, C-index is defined as: \begin{equation}C = \frac{\sum_{a=1}^{n} \sum_{b=0}^{a-1} \sum_{x_i \in L_a} \sum_{x_j \in L_b} \mathbbm{1}(f(x_i) > f(x_j))}{\sum_{a=1}^{n} \sum_{b=0}^{a-1} |L_a| \cdot |L_b|},\end{equation} Similar to the AUROC, a C-index of 1 corresponds to the best model prediction, while a C-index of 0.5 indicates a random prediction.
elements.sent-2411-064: A high C-index score indicates that the model correctly ranks higher-severity anomalies with higher anomaly scores.
elements.sent-2411-065: To further assess the alignment between predicted anomaly scores and severity levels, we employ Kendall's Tau-b.
elements.sent-2411-066: Similar to C-index, Kendall's Tau-b evaluates the consistency between anomaly scores and severity levels but is stricter, as it requires samples within the same severity level to have identical anomaly scores to ensure perfect agreement.
elements.sent-2411-067: Kendall’s Tau ranges from \([-1, 1]\), with \(-1\) indicating a perfectly incorrect ranking, \(0\) signifying no correlation, and \(1\) representing a perfect severity ranking.
elements.sent-2411-068: The formula is detailed in the Appendix.
elements.sent-2411-069: Multilevel anomaly detection has significant implications across real-world domains where distinguishing anomalies based on severity is essential for effective decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-070: One-class Novelty Detection identifies samples that deviate from a defined normal class.
elements.sent-2411-071: For instance, a model trained on Golden Retriever (a dog breed) images can categorize anomalies by their deviation: Level 1 includes other dog breeds (minor deviation); Level 2, cats (moderate similarity); Level 3, birds (significant structural differences); and Level 4, flowers (inanimate objects).
elements.sent-2411-072: This framework enables one-class novelty detection to assign anomaly scores based on the degree of relevance to the trained class.
elements.sent-2411-073: Medical Imaging often involves detecting and assessing anomalies that may significantly impact a patient’s health.
elements.sent-2411-074: Multilevel anomaly detection is valuable as it allows models to differentiate between conditions of varying severity.
elements.sent-2411-075: For instance, in a model trained on images of healthy skin, anomalies can be categorized into levels: Level 1 includes benign lesions, representing minor deviations with no immediate health risk; Level 2 encompasses precancerous lesions, indicating a moderate risk that requires monitoring or intervention; and Level 3 consists of cancerous lesions, severe anomalies demanding urgent medical attention.
elements.sent-2411-076: This structured detection enables healthcare professionals to prioritize high-risk cases, improving patient outcomes by addressing critical conditions promptly.
elements.sent-2411-077: In Industrial Inspection, the economic and operational impact of anomalies can vary widely.
elements.sent-2411-078: Multilevel anomaly detection helps by assessing anomalies not just based on their physical characteristics, but also their potential economic or operational consequences.
elements.sent-2411-079: For example, a small cosmetic defect on a non-essential part may be a low-severity anomaly, while a malfunction in a critical machine component that leads to production downtime or safety hazards would be classified as a high-severity anomaly.
elements.sent-2411-080: This approach ensures that high-risk anomalies that pose significant economic or safety threats are addressed first, optimizing quality control and manufacturing processes.
elements.sent-2411-081: By applying multilevel anomaly detection, real-world applications across these domains benefit from more precise anomaly classification, which enables better prioritization and resource allocation.

elements.sent-2411-082: In our proposed benchmark, we evaluate anomaly detection models across various applications.
elements.sent-2411-083: Among the datasets used, two (DRD-MAD and Covid19-MAD) already include predefined severity labels.
elements.sent-2411-084: For the remaining datasets, which contain class labels for each sample.
elements.sent-2411-085: Classes with ambiguity or samples that do not accurately reflect the assigned class name are excluded from the benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-086: Depending on the application, we categorize anomalies into different numbers of severity levels.
elements.sent-2411-087: The statistics of all datasets can be found in Table \ref{tab:defect-datasets .}
elements.sent-2411-088: Sample distribution across severity levels is detailed in the appendix.
elements.sent-2411-089: We introduce the MultiDogs-MAD dataset to evaluate models' ability to detect anomalies based on class similarity.
elements.sent-2411-090: The training set consists of 500 samples from each of five dog breeds selected from the Stanford Dogs Dataset \cite{dogsdataset : Bichon Frise, Chinese Rural Dog, Golden Retriever, Labrador Retriever, and Teddy.}
elements.sent-2411-091: Each breed in Level 0 is sequentially used as the normal class, with levels 1 to 4 as testing datasets introducing anomalies of increasing severity.
elements.sent-2411-092: Level 0 represents normal samples from the selected breed.
elements.sent-2411-093: Level 1 includes near-distribution anomalies from other dog breeds not in the training set, Level 2 introduces cat images as moderate anomalies \cite{catdataset , Level 3 includes bird images as high-severity anomalies \cite{birddataset , and Level 4 contains flower images as the highest-severity anomalies \cite{flowerdataset , unrelated to animals.}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: Building on the MVTec \cite{bergmann2019mvtec and VisA \cite{zou2022spot datasets, we create two MAD datasets, MVTec-MAD and VisA-MAD, by assigning severity levels to anomalies based on the economic and operational impact of the class to which the anomalies belong.}}
elements.sent-2411-096: Classes that could not be confidently assigned a severity level were excluded.
elements.sent-2411-097: MVTec-MAD includes over 5,073 high-resolution images across fourteen object and texture categories, with defect-free images for training and diverse defects in the test sets.
elements.sent-2411-098: VisA-MAD contains 7,874 images in nine subsets, with 6,405 for training and 1,469 for testing.
elements.sent-2411-099: Both datasets include four severity levels: Level 0 (non-defect), Level 1 (minor defects that are easily repairable), Level 2 (moderate defects with some economic impact), and Level 3 (severe defects with high economic impact).
elements.sent-2411-100: The DRD-MAD is derived from the Diabetic Retinopathy Detection dataset \cite{diabetic-retinopathy-detection , contains high-resolution retina images labeled on a 0-4 severity scale, where 0 represents no DR and 4 indicates proliferative Dr. The images vary in quality and orientation due to different imaging conditions and camera models.}
elements.sent-2411-101: For training, we randomly select 1000 normal samples (severity 0).
elements.sent-2411-102: Testing includes 700 samples for each severity level (0-4), ensuring balanced evaluation across all stages of the disease.
elements.sent-2411-103: The Covid19-MAD is sourced from COVID-19 Severity Scoring \cite{danilov2022dataset , contains 1,364 chest X-ray images: 580 COVID-19 positive cases and 784 normal cases, with 703 images allocated for training.}
elements.sent-2411-104: Severity scores for positive cases range from 0 (no findings) to 6 (severe, above 85\% lung involvement), sourced from four public datasets and annotated by two independent radiologists.
elements.sent-2411-105: The SkinLesion-MAD is designed to evaluate models in detecting and classifying skin anomalies across varying severity levels.
elements.sent-2411-106: It includes 500 training and 500 testing images of healthy skin sourced from Kaggle \cite{kaggle_skin .}
elements.sent-2411-107: Anomalous images from the ISIC Challenge 2018 dataset \cite{codella2019skin, tschandl2018ham10000 , labeled by two doctors through consensus, are divided into Benign lesions (e.g., Melanocytic nevus, Vascular lesions, 652 samples), Precancerous lesions (Actinic keratosis, 327 samples), and Cancerous lesions (Melanoma, 500 samples).}
elements.sent-2411-108: We include baselines of several types, including one-class classification, knowledge distillation-based, reconstruction-based, memory-bank, and distribution map-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-109: The methods we evaluate are Skip-GAN \cite{akccay2019skip , OCR-GAN \cite{liang2023omni , AE4AD \cite{cai2024rethinking , IGD \cite{chen2022deep , RD4AD \cite{deng2022anomaly , RRD \cite{tien2023revisiting , PatchCore \cite{roth2022towards , PNI \cite{bae2023pni , CFLOW-AD \cite{gudovskiy2022cflow and SPR \cite{shin2023anomaly .}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: For all baselines, we strictly follow the original settings, including all hyperparameters such as the learning rate, number of training epochs, optimizer, and others.
elements.sent-2411-112: For new datasets, we apply baseline settings from similar datasets.
elements.sent-2411-113: For each model, we derive the sample-level anomaly score based on its design and use it to evaluate performance.

elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.49\textwidth]{figs/MLLMs_multiAD_2.jpg} \caption{ MLLM-based baselines enable few-shot multilevel AD by leveraging their domain knowledge without fine-tuning, unlike conventional baselines requiring target data training. } \label{fig:MLLMs-based}
elements.sent-2411-114: We acknowledge that assigning anomaly scores corresponding to severity levels requires domain knowledge, which conventional baselines lack.
elements.sent-2411-115: Researchers have recently explored MLLMs for zero-shot learning \cite{anovl, Jiang2024MMADTF or few-shot learning \cite{li2024promptad, zhu2024toward , but these methods do not include the specific context of multilevel anomaly detection.}}
elements.sent-2411-116: Thus, we introduce MLLM-based baselines tailored to the MAD setting.
elements.sent-2411-117: We adopt a few-shot setting to balance effectiveness and cost, by utilizing four images: three normal images and one inference image.
elements.sent-2411-118: We prompt the model with the necessary application context, and instruct it to compare the inference image with the normal images.
elements.sent-2411-119: Based on this comparison, the model generates an anomaly score ranging from 0 to 100, accompanied by reasons, with higher scores indicating greater severity.
elements.sent-2411-121: We evaluate the approach using four state-of-the-art MLLMs: GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, Claude-3.5-Sonnet, and Claude-3.0-Haiku.
elements.sent-2411-122: Additional details on the experimental setup and prompts are provided in the appendix.
elements.sent-2411-123: We conduct experiments to answer the research questions:.
elements.sent-2411-124: RQ1: (Benchmark and Model Types Analysis) How accurately can different types of anomaly detection models assign anomaly scores that align with severity levels across various applications?.
elements.sent-2411-125: RQ2: (Binary-Multilevel Performance Correlation) Does a model that performs well in binary anomaly detection also perform well in multilevel anomaly detection?.
elements.sent-2411-126: RQ3: (Anomalous Area Effect) How does the area of anomalies affect their anomaly score?.
elements.sent-2411-127: RQ4: (Detection Performance Across Severity) How does binary detection performance vary across different severity levels of anomalies ?.
elements.sent-2411-128: RQ5: (Normal Class Expansion) How do the models perform when light anomalies are considered acceptable and included as part of the normal class?.
elements.sent-2411-129: RQ6: (Robustness Analysis) How robust are detection models in aligning anomaly scores with severity under data corruption?.

elements.sent-2411-130: We conducted a comprehensive benchmark across six datasets: MultiDogs-MAD, MVTec-MAD, VisA-MAD, DRD-MAD, Covid19-MAD, and SkinLesion-MAD, evaluating models on three metrics: AUROC (binary AD), C-index, and Kendall's Tau-b.
elements.sent-2411-131: Table \ref{tab:benchmark shows results averaged across dataset subsets.}
elements.sent-2411-132: Detailed results are in the appendix.
elements.sent-2411-133: Conventional models perform reasonably well in multilevel AD tasks on four datasets: MultiDogs-MAD, MVTec-MAD, Visa-MAD, and SkinLesion-MAD, with state-of-the-art methods achieving C scores above 80\%.
elements.sent-2411-134: However, these models struggle on the two medical datasets, DRD-MAD and Covid19-MAD, where C scores are around 65\%.
elements.sent-2411-135: This suggests that multilevel AD for medical images, such as X-rays and retinal fundus images, presents greater challenges for conventional models.
elements.sent-2411-136: Among conventional approaches, knowledge distillation (e.g., RRD, RD4AD) and memory bank-based methods (e.g., PatchCore, PNI) demonstrated higher overall performance.
elements.sent-2411-137: Other approaches, particularly reconstruction-based models, show lower performance in both binary detection and severity level alignment.
elements.sent-2411-138: Most MLLM-based models (except MMAD-Haiku) demonstrated consistently better performance than conventional methods across all datasets, except the VisA-MAD dataset.
elements.sent-2411-139: They achieved both high AUC values and stronger alignment between anomaly scores and severity levels.
elements.sent-2411-140: For instance, in MultiDogs-MAD, MVTec-MAD and SkinLesion-MAD, these models not only matched conventional models in binary detection but also excelled in severity differentiation, as reflected in higher C and Ken values.
elements.sent-2411-141: A notable challenge is observed with the DRD-MAD dataset, which focuses on diabetic retinopathy detection.
elements.sent-2411-142: This dataset is particularly difficult for all models due to its requirement for expert-level knowledge to accurately assess severity levels.
elements.sent-2411-143: Generally, most MLLM-based models outperformed conventional models in multilevel anomaly detection by effectively leveraging domain knowledge, multimodal reasoning, and contextual information to better understand anomaly characteristics and severity.
elements.sent-2411-144: However, MLLM-based models further outperform these methods, highlighting the importance of prior knowledge in multilevel anomaly detection.. \end{tcolorbox.}
elements.sent-2411-145: Since most prior evaluations in anomaly detection focus on the binary setting, we investigate how model performance under the binary setting correlates with performance under our proposed multilevel setting.
elements.sent-2411-146: To this end, we compute the average scores for each metric across all datasets and rank the baselines accordingly, as shown in Table~\ref{tab:benchmark (a lower average rank indicates better performance).}
elements.sent-2411-147: We then calculate Spearman correlation coefficients \cite{zar2005spearman of 0.973 between AUC and C, and 0.916 between AUC and Ken, indicating a strong positive correlation between binary and multilevel performance metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-148: However, despite this overall strong correlation, certain models exhibit notable discrepancies.
elements.sent-2411-149: For most models, the rank differences between AUC and Ken or AUC and C remain within 2, but MMAD-Haiku shows a rank difference of 4, and PNI has a difference of 3.
elements.sent-2411-150: Besides, the binary detection rankings of MLLM-based baselines are consistently higher (worse) than their multilevel detection rankings.
elements.sent-2411-151: This suggests that while MLLM-based models excel in aligning anomaly scores with severity levels (multilevel detection), their binary anomaly detection performance is comparatively weaker.
elements.sent-2411-152: These observations show that binary evaluation does not fully reflect multilevel performance, underscoring the need for our benchmark.

elements.sent-2411-153: We hypothesize that the area of the anomalous region may affect the anomaly score generated by detection models, as these models often rely on differences in spatial features to identify anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-154: To validate this hypothesis, we conduct experiments using the MVTec-MAD and VisA-MAD datasets, as both include ground truth masks for the anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-155: The masks are resized to a width of 256 pixels while maintaining the aspect ratio.
elements.sent-2411-156: We then calculate the anomaly area for each sample and use it as the basis for defining an area-based severity level.
elements.sent-2411-157: Table \ref{tab:area-based presents the multilevel anomaly detection performance for both risk-based severity levels (as used in our benchmark) and area-based severity levels.}
elements.sent-2411-158: The experimental results indicate a strong correlation between the area of anomalous regions and anomaly scores.
elements.sent-2411-159: This is evidenced by the C values for area-based severity levels, which consistently exceed 80\% and are always higher than the C values for risk-based severity levels across both MVTec-MAD and VisA-MAD, with the exception of the MLLM-based baselines.
elements.sent-2411-160: Notably, models such as RD4AD, PatchCore, RRD, and PNI achieve exceptionally high C scores on VisA.
elements.sent-2411-161: These results suggest a bias in conventional models toward the area of anomalous regions.
elements.sent-2411-162: This bias can be advantageous in applications where severity levels are strongly correlated with the area of anomalous regions, such as surface scratches, where a larger area generally indicates greater severity.
elements.sent-2411-163: However, it can underestimate the severity of anomalies with small areas but significant impact, such as a severed electrical wire.

elements.sent-2411-164: A more severe anomaly is intuitively more noticeable to humans.
elements.sent-2411-165: We investigate whether models follow this intuition by examining their performance across increasing severity levels.
elements.sent-2411-166: Specifically, we calculate the AUC between the normal class and each severity level.
elements.sent-2411-167: Results are reported only for MultiDogs-MAD, VisA-MAD, DRD-MAD, and Covid19-MAD in Figure \ref{fig:increaseleve , as performance on MVTec-MAD and SkinLesion-MAD is nearly flawless across all severity levels.}
elements.sent-2411-168: Generally, most models exhibit an upward trend in detection performance as severity levels increase, indicating that higher-severity anomalies are detected more effectively.
elements.sent-2411-169: However, this trend is inconsistent at adjacent lower levels.
elements.sent-2411-170: Specifically, while the performance of most models at the highest levels across all datasets consistently surpasses that at lower levels, models such as PNI and OCR-GAN show inconsistencies in achieving better performance as the severity level increases at adjacent lower levels (e.g., Levels 1, 2, and 3).
elements.sent-2411-171: Yet, fluctuations are observed at lower severity levels in certain cases.. \end{tcolorbox.}
elements.sent-2411-172: In practice, light anomalies are often considered acceptable and may be treated as part of the normal class.
elements.sent-2411-173: For example, a model trained on images of healthy skin without any abnormalities might encounter an inference image with a benign mole, which users want to consider as normal.
elements.sent-2411-174: To replicate this, during test time, we expand the definition of the normal class by progressively including samples from abnormal classes as normal based on severity levels.
elements.sent-2411-175: It is important to note that during the training phase, we exclusively use normal samples (level 0).
elements.sent-2411-176: Figure \ref{fig:classexpand shows that all models experience a performance decline as the normal class expands to include light anomalies, except for MMAD-4o, which maintains stability on several datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-177: This decline occurs because many models assign similarly high anomaly scores to light and serious anomalies, hindering adaptation to the expanded normal class.
elements.sent-2411-178: MMAD-4o's robustness stems from its ability to distinguish between light and severe anomalies.
elements.sent-2411-179: For DRD-MAD, the upward trend is due to the nature of light anomalies, which are difficult to distinguish from normal samples.
elements.sent-2411-180: Models often assign low anomaly scores to these anomalies, treating them as normal.
elements.sent-2411-181: This behavior allows models to adapt more effectively when light anomalies are included in the normal class, maintaining detection performance.

elements.sent-2411-182: To evaluate the robustness of anomaly detection models under corrupted input data, we use C-index on three datasets (MultiDogs-MAD, MVTec-MAD, and SkinLesion-MAD) under two types of input corruptions \cite{hendrycks2019robustness, cao2023anomaly : brightness adjustment and noise addition.}

elements.sent-2411-184: This is because noise can be misinterpreted by the models as defects, leading to a significant drop in C-index.
elements.sent-2411-185: In contrast, models evaluated on the MultiDogs-MAD are less affected by both types of corruption, due to the dataset's characteristic as a one-class novelty detection application, which encourages the models to rely on abstract features rather than fine-grained features.
elements.sent-2411-186: A promising direction for multilevel anomaly detection lies in integrating MLLMs with conventional approaches to leverage their complementary strengths.
elements.sent-2411-187: MLLMs excel in aligning anomaly scores with severity levels through domain knowledge and contextual reasoning, while conventional models perform better in binary detection, particularly for industrial inspection.
elements.sent-2411-188: A hybrid approach, such as a two-stage framework where conventional models detect anomalies and MLLMs assign severity scores, offers a robust and comprehensive solution by combining high accuracy in detection with contextual understanding for severity assessment.
elements.sent-2411-189: In this paper, we introduce Multilevel Anomaly Detection (MAD), a novel setting that emphasizes the alignment of anomaly scores with practical severity.
elements.sent-2411-190: To support this setting, we develop MAD-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark that evaluates the ability of models to reflect severity in diverse real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-191: Through extensive analysis, we provide various findings, revealing insights into the relationship between binary and multilevel detection performance, as well as model robustness.
elements.sent-2411-192: These contributions highlight the potential for MAD to bridge the gap between theoretical anomaly detection and its practical deployment, paving the way for more effective and context-aware AD systems in real-world domains.
elements.sent-2411-193: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUROC) \cite{hanley1982meaning in the context of Multilevel Anomaly Detection (MAD) is defined as: \begin{equation}AUROC = \frac{\sum_{a=1}^{n} \sum_{x_i \in L_a} \sum_{x_j \in L_0} \mathbbm{1}(f(x_i) > f(x_j))}{\sum_{a=1}^{n} |L_a| \cdot |L_0|},\end{equation} \text{where:.}}
elements.sent-2411-194: AUROC achieves a perfect score of 1 if all the anomaly scores of abnormal samples are greater than all the anomaly scores of normal samples.
elements.sent-2411-195: The C-index \cite{uno2011c is a generalization of the AUROC that can evaluate how well anomaly scores align with the severity levels.}
elements.sent-2411-196: In the context of MAD, C-index is defined as: \begin{equation}C = \frac{\sum_{a=1}^{n} \sum_{b=0}^{a-1} \sum_{x_i \in L_a} \sum_{x_j \in L_b} \mathbbm{1}(f(x_i) > f(x_j))}{\sum_{a=1}^{n} \sum_{b=0}^{a-1} |L_a| \cdot |L_b|},\end{equation} A C-index of 1 corresponds to the best model prediction, achieved when all samples from higher-severity levels are consistently assigned higher anomaly scores than samples from lower-severity levels or normal samples.
elements.sent-2411-197: A C-index of 0.5 indicates a random prediction.
elements.sent-2411-198: In our paper, in addition to the C-index, we employ Kendall's Tau-b \cite{kendall1938new for evaluating the consistency between anomaly scores and severity levels.}
elements.sent-2411-199: Kendall's Tau-b, a stricter version of Kendall's Tau, is specifically chosen because it accounts for tied ranks, which is critical when samples within the same severity level are expected to have identical anomaly scores if we expect a perfect consistency (i.e., $\tau_b = 1$).
elements.sent-2411-200: Suppose a pair of samples \((x_i, x_j)\) is concordant if it follows the same order in terms of severity levels and anomaly scores.. That is, if:.
elements.sent-2411-201: The severity level of sample \(x_i\) is greater than that of sample \(x_j\), and the anomaly score of \(x_i\) is also greater than that of \(x_j\) or if.
elements.sent-2411-202: The severity level of sample \(x_i\) is less than that of sample \(x_j\), and the anomaly score of \(x_i\) is also less than that of \(x_j\).
elements.sent-2411-203: The pair is discordant if it is in the reverse ordering for severity levels and anomaly score, or the values are arranged in opposite directions.. That is, if:.
elements.sent-2411-204: The severity level of sample \(x_i\) is greater than that of sample \(x_j\), but the anomaly score of \(x_i\) is less than that of \(x_j\) or if.
elements.sent-2411-205: The severity level of sample \(x_i\) is less than that of sample \(x_j\), but the anomaly score of \(x_i\) is greater than that of \(x_j\).
elements.sent-2411-206: The two observations are tied if the severity level of sample \(x_i\) is equal that of \(x_j\) and/or the anomaly score of sample \(x_i\) is equal that of \(x_j\).
elements.sent-2411-207: Kendall's Tau-b is formulated as: \begin{equation}\tau_b = \frac{C - D}{\sqrt{(C + D + X_0)(C + D + Y_0)}},\end{equation} where:.
elements.sent-2411-208: Note that pairs where both the anomaly scores and severity levels are tied (\((XY)_0\)) are excluded from both the numerator and denominator of Kendall's Tau-b.
elements.sent-2411-209: This exclusion ensures that these pairs do not affect the metric's calculation or penalize the performance.
elements.sent-2411-210: The Kendall's Tau-b ensures that tied pairs only on anomaly scores or only on severity levels are not ignored but instead reduce the overall Kendall's Tau-b value to reflect the uncertainty caused by ties.
elements.sent-2411-211: Consequently, \(\tau_b\) will be equal to 1.0 when the ordering of anomaly scores perfectly corresponds to the ordering of severity levels, and all samples within the same severity level have identical anomaly scores.
elements.sent-2411-212: This characteristic makes Kendall's Tau-b particularly suitable in contexts where it is essential to ensure that a sample with a higher anomaly score always corresponds to a higher severity level.
elements.sent-2411-213: In contrast, the C-index can achieve a perfect score even when anomaly scores within the same severity level are not consistent, as it only considers pairwise comparisons across levels.

Unlike minor surface blemishes, this type of structural damage cannot be repaired or salvaged, hence the high anomaly score.} \end{tabular} \caption{The output of MLLM-based baselines on a sample in MVTec-MAD dataset.} \label{table:example_mvtec}

elements.sent-2411-250: After receiving paper reviews, authors may optionally submit a rebuttal to address the reviewers' comments, which will be limited to a {\bf one page PDF file.}
elements.sent-2411-251: Please follow the steps and style guidelines outlined below for submitting your author response.
elements.sent-2411-252: The author rebuttal is optional and, following similar guidelines to previous conferences, is meant to provide you with an opportunity to rebut factual errors or to supply additional information requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-253: It is NOT intended to add new contributions (theorems, algorithms, experiments) that were absent in the original submission and NOT specifically requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-254: You may optionally add a figure, graph, or proof to your rebuttal to better illustrate your answer to the reviewers' comments.
elements.sent-2411-255: Per a passed 2018 PAMI-TC motion, reviewers should refrain from requesting significant additional experiments for the rebuttal or penalize for lack of additional experiments.
elements.sent-2411-256: Authors should refrain from including new experimental results in the rebuttal, especially when not specifically requested to do so by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-257: Authors may include figures with illustrations or comparison tables of results reported in the submission/supplemental material or in other papers.
elements.sent-2411-258: Just like the original submission, the rebuttal must maintain anonymity and cannot include external links that reveal the author identity or circumvent the length restriction.
elements.sent-2411-259: The rebuttal must comply with this template (the use of sections is not required, though it is recommended to structure the rebuttal for ease of reading).
elements.sent-2411-260: Author responses must be no longer than 1 page in length including any references and figures.
elements.sent-2411-261: Overlength responses will simply not be reviewed.
elements.sent-2411-262: This includes responses where the margins and formatting are deemed to have been significantly altered from those laid down by this style guide.
elements.sent-2411-263: Note that this \LaTeX\ guide already sets figure captions and references in a smaller font.
elements.sent-2411-264: All text must be in a two-column format.
elements.sent-2411-265: The total allowable size of the text area is $6\frac78$ inches (17.46 cm) wide by $8\frac78$ inches (22.54 cm) high.
elements.sent-2411-266: Columns are to be $3\frac14$ inches (8.25 cm) wide, with a $\frac{5 {16}$ inch (0.8 cm) space between them.}
elements.sent-2411-267: The top margin should begin 1 inch (2.54 cm) from the top edge of the page.
elements.sent-2411-268: The bottom margin should be $1\frac{1 {8}$ inches (2.86 cm) from the bottom edge of the page for $8.5 \times 11$-inch paper; for A4 paper, approximately $1\frac{5 {8}$ inches (4.13 cm) from the bottom edge of the page.}}
elements.sent-2411-270: It is important for readers to be able to refer to any particular equation.
elements.sent-2411-271: Wherever Times is specified, Times Roman may also be used.
elements.sent-2411-272: Main text should be in 10-point Times, single-spaced.
elements.sent-2411-273: Section headings should be in 10 or 12 point Times.
elements.sent-2411-274: All paragraphs should be indented 1 pica (approx.~$\frac{1 {6}$ inch or 0.422 cm).}
elements.sent-2411-275: Figure and table captions should be 9-point Roman type as in \cref{fig:onecol .}
elements.sent-2411-276: List and number all bibliographical references in 9-point Times, single-spaced, at the end of your response.
elements.sent-2411-277: When referenced in the text, enclose the citation number in square brackets, for example~\cite{Alpher05 .}
elements.sent-2411-278: Where appropriate, include the name(s) of editors of referenced books.
elements.figure-2411-027: \fbox{\rule{0pt}{0.5in} \rule{0.9\linewidth}{0pt}} \caption{Example of caption. It is set in Roman so that mathematics (always set in Roman: $B \sin A = A \sin B$) may be included without an ugly clash.} \label{fig:onecol}
elements.sent-2411-279: To avoid ambiguities, it is best if the numbering for equations, figures, tables, and references in the author response does not overlap with that in the main paper (the reviewer may wonder if you talk about \cref{fig:onecol in the author response or in the paper).}
elements.sent-2411-282: Please ensure that any point you wish to make is resolvable in a printed copy of the response.
elements.sent-2411-283: Resize fonts in figures to match the font in the body text, and choose line widths which render effectively in print.
elements.sent-2411-284: Readers (and reviewers), even of an electronic copy, may choose to print your response in order to read it.
elements.sent-2411-285: You cannot insist that they do otherwise, and therefore must not assume that they can zoom in to see tiny details on a graphic.
elements.sent-2411-286: When placing figures in \LaTeX, it is almost always best to use \verb+\includegraphics+, and to specify the figure width as a multiple of the line width as in the example below { .}
